rationale: Standard 2nd order finite difference on a moderate grid yields reasonable accuracy and efficiency, but is outperformed by higher-order or spectral methods for this smooth Poisson problem.
4th order finite difference with a fine grid provides high accuracy (log10 error -6), good robustness, and reasonable efficiency for a linear elliptic PDE.
Linear FEM on a 40x40 mesh is robust and flexible but offers only first-order accuracy, making it less suitable for this smooth problem compared to higher-order or spectral approaches.
Quadratic FEM improves accuracy over linear elements and is efficient for the given degrees of freedom, but still lags behind spectral or high-order FD for this analytic solution.
Spectral Fourier method achieves extremely high accuracy (log10 error -10) and is highly efficient for smooth solutions on a rectangular domain with Dirichlet BCs.
Second-order finite volume with central differences is robust and accurate for elliptic problems, but less efficient and accurate than high-order FD or spectral methods for smooth solutions.
Multigrid accelerates convergence for large FD systems, but the method is still only 2nd order and the accuracy (log10 error -3.5) is lower than high-order or spectral approaches.
hp-FEM with adaptivity achieves high accuracy (log10 error -4.5) with very low memory, making it efficient and robust, though not as accurate as spectral methods for this smooth problem.
Chebyshev spectral method is nearly optimal for smooth solutions with Dirichlet BCs, achieving spectral accuracy (log10 error -10) with moderate computational cost.
Upwind FVM is unnecessary for elliptic (diffusion) problems and results in low accuracy (log10 error -2); it is inefficient and not well-suited for this PDE.
